findings.description: The PDF presents the agenda as a dotted-leader list format (time, topic with dotted line, presenter), while the HTML renders it as a formal table with borders and distinct columns. While the data is the same, the original format was not a bordered table.
findings.evidence: PDF shows agenda items in a free-form list with dotted leaders connecting topics to presenters. HTML shows a structured table with headers (Time, Topic, Presenter) and cell borders.

findings.description: The HTML table adds column headers ('Time', 'Topic', 'Presenter') that do not exist in the source PDF. The PDF presents the agenda as a simple list with dotted leaders, not as a table with labeled columns.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <th>Time</th>, <th>Topic</th>, <th>Presenter</th> — these headers are not present in the PDF. However, they are reasonable structural inferences from the content rather than fabricated factual claims.

findings.description: The PDF agenda is not presented as a table but as a formatted list with dotted leaders. The HTML converts this into a structured table, which is a reasonable transformation but changes the original presentation format.
findings.evidence: PDF shows agenda items as lines with dotted leaders connecting topics to presenters. HTML renders this as a 6-row, 3-column table.

findings.description: The HTML uses both h1 and h2 for what appears as a single centered title block in the PDF. 'Stakeholder Meeting #2' is rendered as h2, which is a reasonable structural choice but the PDF treats the title as a unified block.
findings.evidence:
PDF: 'French Broad Hydrologic Model / Stakeholder Meeting #2' as one title block. HTML:

French Broad Hydrologic Model

Stakeholder Meeting #2

findings.description: The HTML has nested <main> elements — one wrapping the entire body content and another inside it wrapping the table and closing text. This is a structural HTML issue but does not affect content fidelity.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <main> inside <body>, then another <main> after </header>
